coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effects of the alternating application of low and high frequency ultrasound for particle synthesis in microreactors
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Zhengya
Udepurkar, Aniket Pradip
Kuhn, Simon
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasound (US) is a promising method to address clogging and mixing issues in microreactors (MR). So far, low frequency US (LFUS), pulsed LFUS and high frequency US (HFUS) have been used independently in MR for particle synthesis to achieve narrow particle size distributions (PSD). In this work, we critically assess the advantages and disadvantages of each US application method for the case study of calcium carbonate synthesis in an ultrasonic microreactor (USMR) setup operating at both LFUS (61.7 kHz, 8 W) and HFUS (1.24 MHz, 1.6 W). Furthermore, we have developed a novel approach to switch between LFUS and HFUS in an alternating manner, allowing us to quantify the synergistic effect of performing particle synthesis under two different US conditions. The reactor was fabricated by gluing a piezoelectric plate transducer to a silicon microfluidic chip. The results show that independently applying HFUS and LFUS produces a narrower PSD compared to silent conditions. However, at lower flow rates HFUS leads to agglomerate formation, while the reaction conversion is not enhanced due to weak mixing effects. LFUS on the other hand eliminates particle agglomerates and increases the conversion due to the strong cavitation effect. However, the required larger power input leads to a steep temperature rise in the reactor and the risk of reactor damage for long-term operation. While pulsed LFUS reduces the temperature rise, this application mode leads again to the formation of particle agglomerates, especially at low LFUS percentage. The proposed application mode of switching between LFUS and HFUS is proven to combine the advantages of both LFUS and HFUS, and results in particles with a unimodal narrow PSD (one order of magnitude reduction in the average size and span compared to silent conditions) and negligible rise of the reactor temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-frequency ultrasound
Acoustic cavitation
Acoustophoresis
Microreactor clogging
Acoustofluidics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge funding from the Research Foundation - Flanders (FWO) and from the European Research Council under the ERC Starting Grant Agreement n. 677169 –MicroParticleControl.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ultrasonic microreactor and setup. (a) Photograph of the fabricated microreactor. The plate at the bottom connected with two red wires is the piezoelectric US transducer glued to the silicon wafer with the etched microchannel. On the top is a glass cover plate with glued nanoports to provide the fluid connections. (b) Sketch of the microchannel structure in the silicon wafer. (c) Schematic of the experiment setup.

Images of CaCO3 particles in the microchannel under NoUS, HFUS and LFUS conditions. Arrows indicate the position of the cavitation bubbles. These images were taken in the middle of channel 11 (as indicated in Fig. 1(b)) by a high-speed camera connected to the microscope. The objective of the microscope was focused at the center of the fluidic channel by the Z-position controller. The total flow rate of the two reactants injected into the microchannel was 0.2 ml/min, which is lower than the typical particle synthesis flow rates (0.4–1.6 ml/min). Lower flow rate was used in this imaging experiment as the camera was unable to capture clear images for particles flowing at higher velocities due to the limited brightness of the microscope light source. The flow direction is from right to left.
Particle size distribution of the synthesized particles under NoUS, HFUS and LFUS conditions for flow rates of (a) 0.4, (b) 0.8 and (c) 1.6 ml/min.

Mean diameter (D50) and span of the PSD (D90–D10) for the synthesized particles under NoUS, HFUS and LFUS conditions for different flow rates. D10, D50 and D90 correspond to 10%, 50% and 90% of particles under the reported particle size.

Temperature rise in the microreactor on application of different US modes. The reactor is cooled with an overhead air fan. A stream of deionized water with a flow rate of 0.8 ml/min was injected into the reactor.
Particle size distribution of the synthesized particles under NoUS, LFUS and the three modes of pulsed LFUS. The total flow rate of the reactants was 0.8 ml/min.
Particle size distribution of the synthesized particles under NoUS, HFUS, LFUS and the three modes of LFUS & LFUS switch. The total flow rate of the reactants was 0.8 ml/min.

Mean diameter (D50) and span of PSD (D90–D10) for the synthesized particles under pulsed LFUS and LFUS & LFUS switch conditions. The horizontal axis corresponds to the LFUS percentage in the pulsed or switch modes. A LFUS percentage of 100% corresponds to continuous LFUS. A LFUS percentage of 0% for the pulse mode corresponds to NoUS condition. While a LFUS percentage of 0% for the switch mode corresponds to continuous HFUS.
Duration of NoUS (or HFUS) and LFUS in a cycle of pulsed LFUS (or LFUS & HFUS switch) modes. The total duration of the two sequences is equal to the residence time of 20 s.

Mean diameter (D50) and span (D90–D10) of the PSD for the synthesized particles for different US modes. ΔT is the temperature rise of the reactor after five reactor volumes, and experiments are reported for a total flow rate of 0.8 ml/min.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effects of the alternating application of low and high frequency ultrasound for particle synthesis in microreactors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: KU Leuven, Department of Chemical Engineering, Celestijnenlaan 200F, 3001 Leuven, Belgium
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: KU Leuven, Department of Chemical Engineering, Celestijnenlaan 200F, 3001 Leuven, Belgium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasound (US) is a promising method to address clogging and mixing issues in microreactors (MR). So far, low frequency US (LFUS), pulsed LFUS and high frequency US (HFUS) have been used independently in MR for particle synthesis to achieve narrow particle size distributions (PSD). In this work, we critically assess the advantages and disadvantages of each US application method for the case study of calcium carbonate synthesis in an ultrasonic microreactor (USMR) setup operating at both LFUS (61.7 kHz, 8 W) and HFUS (1.24 MHz, 1.6 W). Furthermore, we have developed a novel approach to switch between LFUS and HFUS in an alternating manner, allowing us to quantify the synergistic effect of performing particle synthesis under two different US conditions. The reactor was fabricated by gluing a piezoelectric plate transducer to a silicon microfluidic chip. The results show that independently applying HFUS and LFUS produces a narrower PSD compared to silent conditions. However, at lower flow rates HFUS leads to agglomerate formation, while the reaction conversion is not enhanced due to weak mixing effects. LFUS on the other hand eliminates particle agglomerates and increases the conversion due to the strong cavitation effect. However, the required larger power input leads to a steep temperature rise in the reactor and the risk of reactor damage for long-term operation. While pulsed LFUS reduces the temperature rise, this application mode leads again to the formation of particle agglomerates, especially at low LFUS percentage. The proposed application mode of switching between LFUS and HFUS is proven to combine the advantages of both LFUS and HFUS, and results in particles with a unimodal narrow PSD (one order of magnitude reduction in the average size and span compared to silent conditions) and negligible rise of the reactor temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: The combination of US and flow reactors has gained increasing interest in both academia and industry [1–4]. The internal structure sizes of MR in the range of micrometers to a few millimeters are in the same size range as the cavitation bubbles generated by US. Together with the uniform sound distribution, MR offers a well-defined micro-space to investigate US cavitation phenomena and harness their effects to intensify various chemical and biological processes [5–8]. On the other hand, US could also address the two inherent problems of MRs. Their small internal size provides the significant advantages of a high specific surface area, enhanced heat/mass transfer and intrinsic safety [9–13], but also results in channel clogging for flows containing particles [14–16]. This drawback needs to be addressed as more than 60% of the chemical processes in industry involves solids, such as widely encountered heterogeneous catalytic reactions, crystallization and other material synthesis processes [17–19]. Another issue is the weak convective mixing as laminar flow prevails in MRs which are normally operated at low Reynolds numbers [20,21]. MRs with passive mixing structures (such as bends, necks and baffles) in the channel can generate local vortices to improve the mixing, which is however highly dependent on the flow rate (and thus the residence time), resulting in poor operating flexibility [22–24]. Besides, such mixing structures also increase the susceptibility of channel clogging [15]. Integrating MR with ultrasound (US), a safe, noninvasive and environmentally benign external field has been identified as one of the most promising methods to solve the clogging and mixing issues [14,25].

It has been reported that LFUS (20–200 kHz) generates cavitation micro-bubbles which dance and oscillate violently in the liquid, giving rise to acoustic streaming, liquid jets and shock waves in the microchannel [24,26]. These physical effects intensify mixing [24,27] and interfacial mass transfer [28–31], break up agglomerates [32,33] and detach particles deposited on the channel wall, thus preventing clogging in MR [34–36]. Moreover, the mixing performance in USMR is mainly controlled by the US parameters and less dependent on flow rate, resulting in a wide operating range of flow rate and processing time [24]. Despite these advantages, one major problem of LFUS is that continuous sonication leads to a considerable temperature increase in the reactor due to its large power input (in the range of 10–1000 W) [25,34]. Such high energy consumption and temperature rise inhibits the scale-up and industrial application of US [37].

One method to reduce the power consumption of LFUS is applying it in a pulse mode. It has been reported that applying pulsed US with an optimized ON/OFF cycle time doesn’t reduce (and in some cases even enhances) the cavitation activity compared to continuous mode [38–41]. Gielen et al. [42] investigated the effect of a pulsed ultrasonic field of 30 kHz on the cooling crystallization of paracetamol in a batch and a recycled flow crystallizer. Similar nucleation temperatures and particle sizes compared to continuous sonication were observed in both setups when US was active for 10% of the time, reducing the power consumption by 90%. Delacour et al. [43] reported an USMR consisting of a plate MR attached to a Langevin-type transducer working at a frequency of 21 kHz and a power of 11 W. They observed that applying US for only 12.5% of the residence time was sufficient to prevent microchannel clogging for the synthesis of barium sulfate particles. Reducing the effective US treatment ratio from 100% to 12.5% decreased the temperature rise of the reactor from 7 K to less than 1 K after 5 reactor volumes.

Another way to improve the energy efficiency of US is to utilize the acoustophoretic effect [44] at high US frequency (1–100 MHz). As HFUS is operated at power levels below the cavitation threshold, cavitation effects are normally not observed while the effect of acoustic radiation force arises especially when a standing wave is formed in the fluid channel. The strong radiation force generated by the standing wave could drive particles to the pressure node [45,46]. This principle has been successfully implemented in MR, whose channel size is in the range of the wavelength for HFUS (15 µm-1.5 mm for 1–100 MHz US in water). This well-established area is known as acoustophoresis and has been widely applied to cell and particle separation, concentration and manipulation in microfluidic chips [47–49]. However, besides these biological and chemical analysis applications, very few studies have been devoted to chemical synthesis processes [50]. Sriphutkiat et al. [51] investigated the effect of standing surface acoustic waves on the accumulation and clogging of SiO2 particles in a 100 μm microchannel. The HFUS has a frequency of 19.95 MHz and a power from 0.3 to 1 W. They found that the acoustophoretic effect reduced the accumulation area by 2–3.7 fold and thus delayed clogging. Dong et al. [50] recently extended the application of acoustophoresis to material synthesis processes. They developed an HFUS microreactor with a half wavelength standing wave formed in both the depth and width direction of the microchannel, which focused the particles to the node point at the channel center. This focusing effect not only prevented solid deposition (and thus clogging) on the channel wall, but also induced a more uniform velocity distribution for the particles, resulting in a narrower particle size distribution for both CaCO3 and BaSO4 synthesis processes. The reactor worked at a resonance frequency of 1.21 MHz and input power range of 0.3–3.3 W. Due to the low power input, no active cooling was needed to control the reactor temperature.

Although both pulsed LFUS and HFUS have been employed to prevent clogging and control particle synthesis process at low energy consumption, it is difficult to exactly compare their performance as the reported research was conducted in different reactors with different reaction conditions. In this paper, a USMR setup that could work at both low and high frequency was built. The effect of LFUS, HFUS and pulsed LFUS on the particle size distribution, conversion and temperature rise for a model particle synthesis process was investigated and compared. Both advantages and disadvantages were identified for LFUS, pulsed LFUS and HFUS. A novel method to switch between LFUS and HFUS was proposed to combine the advantages of both. Applying dual- or multiple-frequency US waves simultaneously or consecutively has been used to enhance the cavitation effect [52–54]. Two (or multiple) US waves will interact with each other, improving the acoustic field uniformity and increasing the total number of cavitation bubbles. Feng et al. [55] reported that combined irradiation of low frequency (28 kHz) and high frequency (0.87–1.87 MHz) US waves at high intensity (1–10 W/cm2) produced a cavitation yield which is increased by a factor of 1.3–3.4 compared to the sum of the yields given separately by single frequency irradiation. This enhanced effect is explained by the fact that the bubbles generated at high frequency act as nuclei for low frequency cavitation and vice versa [56,57]. In contrast to these studies which combine the cavitation effect of both LFUS and HFUS, we propose to integrate the cavitation effect of LFUS with the acoustophoretic effect of low intensity HFUS to synergistically intensify material synthesis processes. Moreover, the combination of LFUS and HFUS is achieved by a simple setup consisting of a piezo transducer, a dual-channel signal generator, a power amplifier and a switch device, compared to above mentioned studies which either require two transducers or two sets of signal generator and amplifier to generate the dual-frequency US field.

The structure of the USMR and the experiment setup is shown in Fig. 1 . The reactor is a scaled-up version of the HFUS acoustophoretic reactor described in our previous work [50]. The reactor materials and dimensions were designed according to the principle of a layered acoustic resonator [58,59]. The reactor consists of three layers, i.e. a piezo plate, a silicon layer and a glass cover plate, and is designed for a fundamental resonance frequency of 1.21 MHz. A standing wave is created in the thickness direction of each reactor layer, which minimizes the loss of acoustic energy and maximizes the acoustic pressure amplitude in channel. In transversal direction there is another standing wave of 12 wavelengths (the 11 straight channels are uniformly distributed in the reactor width direction with a spacing of one wavelength). These resonance modes in both the thickness and transversal direction generate a two-dimensional half standing wave in the square channel, which focusses the particles in the center line of the channel. The reactor has an overall size of 77 × 40 × 4.56 mm3, and the meandering square channel (0.6 × 0.6 mm2) was fabricated in the silicon plate by deep reactive ion etching. The glass cover plate was anodically bonded to the silicon plate to seal the channel. The piezoelectric plate transducer (80 × 40 × 1.67 mm3 Pz26, Ferroperm, Denmark) was glued to the back of the silicon plate by epoxy glue (301, Epotek). The total channel length is 725.8 mm and the reactor volume 261.3 µL. The reactor has three inlets and one outlet. A nanoport was glued to each inlet/outlet. The inlets were connected to the syringe pump (Fusion 200, KR Analytical) by PFA tubing (OD 1/16″, ID 1 mm). The outlet was connected to a short PFA tubing (OD 1/16″, ID 1 mm) of approximately 70 mm in length and 5.5 µL in volume, which is 2.1% of the reactor volume (and thus its influence on the experiment results is neglected). To investigate the influence of US on the fluid dynamics and particle movement in the MR, the microchannel was observed by a multifunctional stereo microscope (SZ25 Nikon) equipped with a high-speed camera (Mini UX1000, Photron) and a controller to accurately position the objective. Inert polystyrene particles were first injected into the microchannel to check the focusing performance of this HFUS acoustophoretic reactor. Results show that the optimum particle focusing frequency is 1.24 MHz (see Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Material), which is close to the design frequency of 1.21 MHz.

Although the reactor is initially designed for HFUS acoustophoretic focusing, it can also work at LFUS with a lower efficiency. The measured admittance curve (see Fig. S2 in the Supplementary Material) shows that there is a resonance peak around 61.7 kHz which probably corresponds to a bending vibration mode of the reactor in the length or width direction [60]. For all the experiment results discussed in this paper, the frequency and net power applied was 1.24 MHz and 1.6 W for HFUS, and 61.7 kHz and 8 W for LFUS. The average US power intensity on the transducer surface is approximately 0.05 W/cm2 for HFUS and 0.25 W/cm2 for LFUS. To generate the US, the piezo transducer was actuated by a power amplifier (E&I, RF 2100L, 100 W) driven by a sine wave from a dual-channel signal generator (DG1032Z, Rigol). For modes of pulsed LFUS and LFUS & HFUS switch, a single-pole double-throw (SPDT) radio frequency switch device (USB-1SPDT-A18, Mini Circuits) was used to switch between the two output channels of the signal generator, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The output port of the switch was connected to the power amplifier. For LFUS & HFUS switch mode, one channel of the signal generator was set to LF condition and the other to HF condition (with the HF channel turned off for the pulsed LFUS experiments). The switch sequence between NoUS (or HFUS) and LFUS was set by the device software. A cycle time of 20 s that equals the residence time of the reactor (at a flow rate of 0.8 ml/min) was chosen. Within one US cycle, these 20 s were split between a LFUS sequence and a following NoUS (or HFUS) sequence, see Table 1 . The duration of LFUS in the three pulsed LFUS (or LFUS & HFUS switch) modes is 2.5 s, 5 s and 7.5 s respectively, yielding a lower percentage of LFUS application time (12.5–37.5%) than NoUS (or HFUS). The temperature change of the reactor was monitored using a thermocouple (HH374, Omega) connected at the reactor outlet via a T-junction. The reactor itself was cooled by an air fan (Nidec TA600, 32 W).

The synthesis of CaCO3 is carried out in the reactor by mixing the two aqueous solutions of sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, Accros Organics, 99.5% purity, 16 mM) and calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O, Sigma Aldrich, 99% purity, 16 mM) prepared with milli-Q water. The CaCl2 solution is injected through inlet 1 and Na2CO3 through inlet 2 with equal flow rate for all the experiments (the unused inlet 3 was blocked). The total flow rate applied was 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6 ml/min, corresponding to a residence time of 10, 20 and 40 s in the reactor respectively. After an average stabilization time of 5 reactor volumes, the slurry at the reactor outlet was directly collected in the dispersion cell (Hydro SV, Malvern) of a Malvern 3000 laser sizer and then immediately analysed for the PSD. A sample volume of 1.2 ml was collected in the cell which was initially filled with approximately 6 ml of milli-Q water, in order to dilute the reactants and minimize further reaction during the analysis process. Each condition was measured three times and only the average PSD result was used. To quantify the conversion of the reaction, the conductivity of the solution at the reactor outlet was measured and then related to the conversion by a calibration curve. The calibration curve was obtained by measuring the conductivity of calibration solutions whose concentration of ions are equal to that of the actual outlet solution at a particular conversion (see section S3 in the Supplementary Material for details). After each experiment, the reactor channels were thoroughly cleaned with dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl) followed by milli-Q water to remove any CaCO3 particles remaining on the channel walls.

The precipitation of calcium carbonate (CaCO3) particles was studied as a model material synthesis process. Calcium carbonate is an important filler material widely used for paints, pigments, coatings, paper, plastics etc. [61,62] Such industrial applications always require well-defined powders with a narrow size distribution and uniform shape [63–65]. However, due to the laminar flow profile in the MR, the wide residence time distribution results in a broad particle size distribution and the weak mixing leads to low conversion [50,66]. Improving the size distribution and conversion of CaCO3 by applying different US modes will be discussed.

By focusing the particles in the channel center via the acoustophoretic forces of HFUS, the particles will experience a shorter and more uniform growth time, resulting in a smaller average size and a narrower size distribution [50]. This effect on the CaCO3 synthesis process was further investigated in this study. From the images of the particle distribution in the channel shown in Fig. 2 , it is seen that in silent condition large agglomerates are formed in the channel. With HFUS active, the particles are focused in the center and have a smaller and more uniform size. This observation is further confirmed by the narrow PSD obtained under HFUS as shown in Fig. 3 . Without sonication, the particle size distribution is bimodal, and consists of a population of primary particles in the size range of 0.3–10 μm and a population of secondary particles in the range of 10–300 μm. This bimodal distribution of CaCO3 precipitates has also been reported in other studies [50,62–64]. The first peak mainly represents individual particles formed by nucleation and growth, whereas the secondary peak represents agglomerates [50,62]. Upon application of HFUS, the amount of agglomerates in the second peak was reduced, leading to more primary particles. As a result, the average size and span of the distribution under HFUS were much lower than NoUS, as show in Fig. 4 . These effects are extremely significant at higher flow rates of 0.8 and 1.6 ml/min. At a flow rate of 0.4 ml/min, the amount of agglomerates is larger compared to other flow rates. This is explained by the fact that the particles stay longer in the channel compared to their focussing time, and thus agglomeration or clustering between the particles in the focus band is promoted [50]. This phenomenon has been widely observed in acoustophoretic particle trapping [49] and sedimentation enhancement [67] experiments. Thus, it can be concluded that for the HFUS assisted material synthesis there is an optimum ratio of residence time and focusing time, which restricts the achievable flow rate range. Another drawback of HFUS is its relatively weak mixing effect compared to LFUS. Although Rayleigh streaming is always accompanied with a standing wave, its velocity is normally in the range of 0.01–1 mm/s [7,45], which is several orders of magnitude lower than the convective flow velocity (18–74 mm/s) in our experiment. Therefore, the effect of this acoustic streaming on mixing is limited. This explains that the conversion obtained at flow rate of 0.8 ml/min under HFUS (57 ± 2%) is equal to that under NoUS condition (58 ± 2%).

Contrastingly to HFUS, the application of LFUS is known for its strong effect on agglomerate fragmentation [32,33] and mixing improvement induced by cavitation phenomena such as microstreaming, liquid jets and shock waves [24,26,68]. LFUS has been widely used to improve particle size uniformity, improve conversion and prevent clogging for material synthesis processes in MR [34,36,69]. These effects were also observed in our experiments. As shown in Fig. 2, the LFUS generated cavitation bubbles enhance mixing and circulate the particles in the reactor channel. No agglomerates and only fine particles were observed. The effect of LFUS on the particle size is demonstrated more clearly in Fig. 3. It is seen that for all the three flow rates, PSD under LFUS is almost unimodal with more than 97% volume of the particles lying in the primary particle size range. This results in almost one order of magnitude reduction of the average size and span compared to NoUS condition, as shown in Fig. 4. Moreover, due to the strong mixing effect of the cavitation phenomena, the conversion under LFUS was improved by 14% to a value of 72 ± 2% compared to silent condition, as shown in Table 2 .

In conclusion, compared with HFUS, LFUS eliminates particle agglomerates, and produces a unimodal and narrow PSD and increases the conversion. However, the necessary US power to achieve these results is much larger for LFUS, corresponding to 8 W compared to 1.6 W for HFUS. The temperature rise in the reactor was so steep while applying LFUS that it was difficult to control the reactor temperature via air fan cooling, as shown in Fig. 5 . The temperature increases 5.6 K after five reactor volume (100 s), which is eight times the value observed for HFUS (0.7 K). Such a large temperature rise without effective control will influence the experimental results and also damage the reactor, especially the nanoport connections at the inlet/outlet.

A possible approach to limit the temperature rise associated with LFUS while still harnessing its advantages is the application of LFUS in pulse mode [42,43]. Pulsed LFUS involves applying LFUS for a period followed by a period of no US (NoUS) application. During the actuation of LFUS, the cavitation phenomena promotes mixing and breaks up particle agglomerates, and in the absence of LFUS the reactor can cool down. It enables particle synthesis at a lower power input and thus with improved temperature control. It was reported that the ON/OFF cycle time for pulsed US has significant influence on the cavitation activity and need to be optimized for a specific application [40–42]. In our experiments, three pulse modes corresponding to 2.5, 5 and 7.5 s of LFUS in each cycle (20 s) were investigated, as shown in Tables 1 and 2. The average power input for the three modes ranges between 1.0 and 3.0 W, which is much lower compared to continuous LFUS (8.0 W). As a result, the reactor temperature for the pulse mode of No15-LF5 increases slowly over time and becomes stable after three reactor volumes while cooling with an air fan (Fig. 5), resulting in a maximum temperature rise of 1 K, which is much lower than that of continuous LFUS. Due to the absence of LFUS for some periods (62.5–87.5%) in the pulse mode, the conversion is 7–9% lower than in the continuous LFUS mode (see Table 2), but still 5–7% larger compared to the NoUS mode.

The influence of pulsed LFUS on the PSD is shown in Fig. 6
. Similarly to continuous LFUS, the pulse modes are also effective in reducing the amount of agglomerates, which is 6.3–13.5% compared to 83% for the NoUS case. Thus, short pulses of LFUS are effective in synthesizing particles with narrow PSD. As shown in Fig. 8 , both the average size and span of the distribution were significantly reduced under pulsed LFUS compared to NoUS. With increasing percentage of LFUS in the pulse mode, the average size and span decrease and approach the case of continuous LFUS mode. However, as shown in Fig. 6 and Table 2, there are still some agglomerates in the synthesized CaCO3 under pulsed LFUS in our experiment. This agglomerate amount increases with decreasing LFUS percentage, as in the longer NoUS periods the particles will grow non-uniformly, settle down and attach to the wall, promoting agglomerate formation in the channel. Increasing the NoUS percentage will thus promote this agglomeration tendency and correspondingly decrease the agglomerate breaking effect of LFUS.

To prevent the agglomerate formation in the NoUS period of the pulsed LFUS mode, we replace the NoUS period by HFUS, resulting in a switch mode between HFUS and LFUS. As discussed earlier, HFUS is able to focus the particles in the channel center, resulting in formation of smaller particles and preventing particle attachment to the wall. Moreover, with the input power of HFUS is much lower than LFUS, substituting the NoUS period by HFUS maintains a low power consumption and thus a low reactor temperature rise. To verify this concept, three switch modes between LFUS and HFUS corresponding to a sequence of 2.5, 5 and 7.5 s of LFUS and subsequently 17.5, 15 and 12.5 s of HFUS in each cycle were applied. On activation of LFUS, the particles experience effects of cavitation and on switching to HFUS, the acoustophoretic forces focus the particles to the center of the channel. As shown in Table 2, the average input power for the three switch modes is in the range of 2.4–4.0 W, which is higher than that of the pulse modes (1.0–3.0 W), but still much lower than the continuous LFUS mode (8.0 W). As a result, the temperature rise is slow and can be controlled by the air fan cooling, as shown in Fig. 5. The reactor temperature for the switch mode of HF15-LF5 reaches a stable level after about five reactor volumes, with a maximum temperature rise of 1.4 K, close to that of the pulse mode of No15-LF5 (1 K). The observed reaction conversion is also similar to the pulse mode, which is in line with the earlier assessment that HFUS does not enhance mixing.

However, and most notably, the switch mode between LFUS and HFUS reduces the amount of agglomerates further. As detailed in Table 2, the agglomerate amount for the three switch modes are between 4.5 and 6.7%, significantly lower than that of the pulse modes (between 6.3 and 13.5%) at identical LFUS percentages. This is further confirmed by comparing Figs. 7 and 8, where the secondary peak representing particle agglomerates for the switch modes is much lower and closer to that of LFUS compared to the pulse modes. Consequently, the average size and PSD span for the switch modes are also smaller compared to the pulse modes, as shown in Fig. 8. With increasing LFUS percentage, the mean size (or span) for the switch and pulse modes approach each other and overlap with each other at a LFUS percentage of 37.5%. This implies that the influence of HFUS on the particles size is only significant at lower LFUS percentage. While at higher LFUS percentage, the particle size is dominated by the effect of LFUS and hence replacing the NoUS period in the pulse mode by HFUS won’t improve the size distribution but only increase the power input. In fact, when reducing the LFUS percentage from 37.5% to 12.5%, the span in the switch mode only increases by 0.5 µm (with an almost identical mean size), in contrast to the significant increase (29.2 µm) in pulse mode, as shown in Table 2. This indicates that only 12.5% LFUS in the switch mode (HF17.5-LF2.5) is sufficient to produce a unimodal and narrow PSD close to that of the continuous LFUS mode. While in pulse mode, the required LFUS percentage is 37.5% (No12.5-LF7.5). Remarkably, both the mean size and span for modes HF17.5-LF2.5 and No12.5-LF7.5 are close to that of continuous LFUS, as shown in Table 2, and these PSD are achieved at smaller US power input.

Particle synthesis processes in MR are challenging due to the issues of clogging and weak convective mixing. Both LFUS and HFUS are able to overcome these issues. An USMR setup operating at both low and high frequency was built and the synthesis of calcium carbonate was studied as a model system. HFUS focuses the particles in the channel center via the acoustophoretic forces and results in a narrower PSD compared to the NoUS condition. However, this effect reduces with decreasing flow rate due to the formation of agglomerates in the focus band. Besides, the conversion is not improved by HFUS due to its weak mixing enhancement. Thanks to strong cavitation effects, LFUS breaks up the particle agglomerates and hence results in one order of magnitude reduction in the average size and span of the PSD compared to silent conditions, and an increase of 14% in the reaction conversion. However, these improvements are at the expense of a much larger power input which increases the reactor temperature. The application of pulsed LFUS reduces the overall power input while still harnessing the mixing and aggregate break up effects of LFUS. However, especially at a low LFUS percentage larger agglomerates are observed, which are formed in the NoUS period. Replacing this NoUS period by HFUS, i.e. switching LFUS and HFUS allows to further reduce the amount of agglomerates and results in a narrow PSD at lower power consumption compared to the pulsed LFUS mode. This switch mode synergistically combines the strong cavitation effects of LFUS and the high energy efficient acoustophoretic focusing effect of HFUS. While we chose the synthesis of CaCO3 as model reaction, this novel approach can be adapted and extended to various material synthesis processes. Moreover, the switch between the two US frequencies is realized in the same setup as for single-frequency US application, for which only an additional SPDT switch is required, offering a simple and cost-effective approach to generate dual- or multi-frequency US fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge funding from the Research Foundation - Flanders (FWO) and from the European Research Council under the ERC Starting Grant Agreement n. 677169–MicroParticleControl.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2019.104800.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Merging microfluidics and sonochemistry: towards greener and more efficient micro-sono-reactors
